attributes.stem:
A couple present to their general practitioner to discuss a planned pregnancy. She has not conceived before. She ceased the combined oral contraceptive pill six months ago and since then the couple have been using condoms. They have questions about what kind of supplements she should be taking while trying to conceive. She has no medical history, no significant family history or allergies. She does not take any medications. She is a non-drinker and non smoker and has a normal BMI and balanced diet.

Some dietary supplements are discussed.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is most important?
options: 500 micrograms of folic acid at least 4 weeks prior to and during the first 12 weeks of pregnancy
150 micrograms of iodine while attempting pregnancy and during the first 12 weeks of pregnancy
1000 IU of Vitamin D daily while attempting and during the first 4 weeks of pregnancy
1000mg of calcium per day during the entire pregnancy
5mg of folic acid at least 6 weeks prior to pregnancy and during the first 12 weeks of pregnancy
attributes.explanation: RANZCOG recommends a dose of at least 400micrograms of folic acid to aid in the prevention of neural tube defects (anencephaly, spina bifida) leading up to pregnancy and in the early embryonic period during organogenesis with the central nervous system is developing. Therefore, the following option is the correct answer: 500 micrograms of folic acid at least 4 weeks prior to during the first 12 weeks of pregnancy. Where there is an increased risk of neural tube defects (not applicable in this situation) such as a risk of malabsorption, BMI >30 or previous pregnancies with neural tube defects, a 5mg dose is recommended. Iodine dose of 150 micrograms is also recommended throughout pregnancy and breastfeeding, but is not as important as folic acid in the preconception period. Calcium are also very important in pregnancy but only need to be supplemented if dietary intake is insufficient. Current RANZCOG guidelines suggest women at high risk of Vitamin D deficiency (regular sunscreen use, veiled women, dark skinned women, women with BMI >30) should be tested and supplementation instituted when deficient. Other local guidelines, such as South Australia's Perinatal Practice Guidelines, suggest routine supplementation of all pregnant women with 400IU cholecalciferol daily, with women at high risk of deficiency having a 25-hydroxy vitamin D level tested and deficiency treated with 1000IU daily until 28 weeks, where levels should be re-tested to guide subsequent management. Latest WHO recommendations, however, state that Vitamin D supplementation is not recommended for pregnant women to improve maternal and perinatal outcomes.
learningPoints: Folic acid is very important in the preconception and early pregnancy period for prevention of neural tube defects.
references: Royal College of Obstetricians and Gynaecologists. Vitamin and Mineral Supplementation and Pregnancy. 2015. Available from: https://www.hps.com.au/wp-content/uploads/2019/04/Vitamin-and-mineral-supplementation-in-pregnancy-C-Obs-25-Review-Nov-2014-Amended-May-2015.pdf
Dorney E, Black KI. Preconception care. Australian journal of general practice. 2018;47(7):424–9. Available from: https://www1.racgp.org.au/ajgp/2018/july/preconception-care

SA Health Perinatal Practice Guidelines. Preconception Care. 2015. Available from: https://www.sahealth.sa.gov.au/wps/wcm/connect/1f11de804eed8cb5afbeaf6a7ac0d6e4/Preconception+Advice_Sept2015.pdf?MOD=AJPERES&CACHEID=ROOTWORKSPACE-1f11de804eed8cb5afbeaf6a7ac0d6e4-nGEUhYK
